1. Generating Random Networks¶

(b)¶

In [25]:
from project1_code import question_1b
question_1b()

p=0.002 P(conn)~0.00, avg GCC frac~0.73, avg GCC diam~25.30
p=0.006 P(conn)~0.00, avg GCC frac~1.00, avg GCC diam~8.20
p=0.012 P(conn)~1.00, avg GCC frac~1.00, avg GCC diam~5.00
p=0.045 P(conn)~1.00, avg GCC frac~1.00, avg GCC diam~3.00
p=0.100 P(conn)~1.00, avg GCC frac~1.00, avg GCC diam~3.00
GCC diameter=24 for a disconnected network (p=0.002) instance

All random realizations of the ER network are not connected. As the probability p increases, however, the probability of connectivity increases.

For one instance of a disconnected, randomly generated network with p = 0.002, the GCC diameter = 22. By definition, a Giant Connected Network (GCC) is a subset, or a subgraph, of the graph that we randomly generate and is always connected and hence, a non-infinite and numerical diameter answer.

(c)¶

from project1_code import question_1c
question_1c()

First X value above Y > 0.005: 0.00021
Actual Y value at that point: 0.00513

(d)¶

In [ ]:
from project1_code import question_1d
question_1d(c=0.5)
In [ ]:
question_1d(c=1.0)

ii. The same decreasing exponential trend is observed for c=1, except the GCC size does not approach zero but remains on a higher floor centering 0.04

In [ ]:
from project1_code import question_1d_part_iv
question_1d_part_iv()

Generating random networks for c=1.15
Generating random networks for c=1.25
Generating random networks for c=1.35

iii. See above for c=1.15, 1.25, 1.35 plots.

iv. As c increases, we observe a logarithmic trend. As the number of nodes increase, the GCC size increases and saturates to a steady state value. The steady-state value becomes higher as c, the average degree of nodes, increases. For c=1.15 in particular, the trend line starts to, but not quite yet, resemble a logarithmic pattern, having passed the critical c=1 threshold when many isolated components of the network start to connect. The logarithmic trend is much more evident with c=1.25 and c=1.35.

2. Create networks using preferential attachment model¶

(a)¶

In [ ]:
from project1_code import question_2a
question_2a()

[True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
Yes, this network is always connected

(b)¶

In [ ]:
from project1_code import question_2b
question_2b()

IGRAPH U--- 1050 1049 -- 
connected: True
max degree: 24
mean degree: 1.9980952380952381
degree variance: 4.17332970521542
edges: 1049 , assortativity: -0.09135136092382576 , modularity: 0.935
Assortativity is defined as a measure of the tendency for nodes to connect to other nodes with similar properties
Assortativity is positive if similar nodes tend to connect to each other, and negative otherwise

(c)¶

In [ ]:
from project1_code import question_2c
question_2c()

IGRAPH U--- 10500 10499 -- 
connected: True
max degree: 95
mean degree: 1.9998095238095237
degree variance: 5.846476154195011
edges: 10499 , assortativity: -0.03100035425224403 , modularity: 0.979

The value of assortativity for a 10500 nodes is slightly less negative and the value of modularity is slightly higher than the one with 1050 nodes. However, the change in value isn't that big to claim that there is a significant difference.

(e)¶

rough log-log slope: -1.2256956531976575
In [ ]:
question_2e(10500)

rough log-log slope: -1.4686951209862034

The distribution should still be linear since sampling a neighbor of a random node is still fundamentally drawing from a degree-biased version of the same power-law population. That being said, the slope becomes less negative(or less steep) compared to node degree distribution.

rough log-log slope: -1.1682994301888403

Main observations noted looking at PA networks with different values of m:

  • Modularity decreases as m increases
  • Assortativity value is close to 0 regardless of the value of m
  • Mean degree increases as m increases
  • Plotting the degree distribution of nodes in log-log scale still shows a linear distribution with a slope of around -2 regardless of the value of m
  • Randomly picking a node i and getting the degree of nodes of one if its neighbor j, also shows a linear distribution with a less negative(or less steep) slope regardless of the value of m.

New Network

IGRAPH U--- 1050 1049 -- 
connected: False
max degree: 37
mean degree: 1.9980952380952381
degree variance: 6.457139229024944
edges: 1049 , assortativity: 0.03185862710335647

Comparison:

  • Stub-matching network has a lower modularity than PA method
  • Stub-matching network is also not connected whereas PA is
  • PA network has a negative assortativity, while Stub-matching network is positive(and closer to 0)

2. Random Walk on Networks¶

In [ ]:
from project1_code import question2_1
question2_1()

 (a) Generate ER graph with N = 900 

Nodes     : 900
Edges     : 6062
Diameter  : 5
Simulating 2000 random walk trials

 (b) Average distance and variance 


 (c) Degree distribution comparison 


Average Graph Degree      : 13.471
Average End-Node Degree   : 14.266
The degree distribution of the nodes reached at the end of the random walk is biased toward higher-degree nodes compared to the degree distribution of the original graph. In an unbiased random walk on an undirected network, the probability of visiting a node in the long run is proportional to its degree, because nodes with more incident edges are more likely to be visited.

 (d) Repeat for N = 9000 

Nodes     : 9000
Edges     : 606089
Diameter  : 3
Simulating 1000 random walk trials

Diameter Comparison
N = 900  : 5
N = 9000 : 3
When the network size was increased from 900 to 9000 nodes while keeping the edge probability fixed at p=0.015, the diameter decreased from 5 to 3. Although the larger graph contains ten times as many nodes, it is also much denser because the expected degree increases from approximately 13.5 to 135. The increased connectivity creates many additional shortcuts between nodes, reducing the maximum shortest-path distance in the network.

2. Random walk on networks with fat-tailed degree distribution¶

In [ ]:
from project1_code import question2_2
question2_2()

 (a) Generate PA graph with N = 900, M = 1 

Nodes     : 900
Edges     : 899
Diameter  : 16
Simulating 2000 random walk trials

 (b) Average distance and variance 


 (c) Degree distribution comparison 


Average Graph Degree      : 1.998
Average End-Node Degree   : 4.300
With the original having an average degree of 1.998 and the random having 4.839, there is a large bias to higher degree nodes this is an effect of having a stationary distribution on an undirected network where the probability of visit is directly proportional to its degree. In this graph the vast majority of nodes are leaves with a degree of 1 (over 60%), the random walker drops the probability of landing on degree-1 nodes to under 30%.

 (d) Repeat for N = 9000 

Nodes     : 9000
Edges     : 8999
Diameter  : 28
Simulating 1000 random walk trials

 Repeat for N = 90 

Nodes     : 90
Edges     : 89
Diameter  : 9
Simulating 1000 random walk trials

Diameter Comparison
N = 90  : 9
N = 900  : 16
N = 9000 : 28
PA model fixes m = 1, locking the average degree at a sparse baseline of k ~= 2 for all network  sizes. This network remains sparse as it grows, with the diameter increasing substantially with the number of nodes, expanding from 12 to 27 as grows 90 to 90000, shifting also the avg distance and variance. The expanding diameter also causes the random walker significantly more steps to explore the larger periphery and the distance keeps increasing showing that the system has not yet reached global equilibrium.

3. PageRank¶

(a)¶

In [28]:
from project1_code import question2_3a
question2_3a()

Node with highest in-degree: 394
Node with highest visit probability: 394

The plot above shows that the visit probability is proportional to the in-degree of a node (i.e., number of edges directed into a node). Over a long random walk of 5,000 steps, we observed that the node with the highest in-degree had the highest visit probability.

(b)¶

In [29]:
from project1_code import question2_3b
question2_3b(alpha=0.2, num_steps=int(1e5)) # Lower alpha value

Node with highest in-degree: 394
Node with highest visit probability: 394
alpha=0.2, num_steps=100000
In [30]:
# Higher alpha value
question2_3b(alpha=0.8, num_steps=int(1e5))

Node with highest in-degree: 394
Node with highest visit probability: 394
alpha=0.8, num_steps=100000

For PageRank with teleportation, when (i.e., the probability of teleportation) is low, the network's node degree dictates a node's visit probability, meaning the preferential hubs will have the highest visit probabilities. As shown in the first plot above, there are spikes in the probabilities around the preferential hubs.

When is high, however, the walker teleports constantly and the structure of the network is essentially ignored as the walker is teleported rather than following directed edges. The probability essentially flattens out to a uniform distribution. With 900 nodes, the uniform probability is 1/900 = 0.001, which is reflected in the higher floor centered around this value in the second graph above.

4. Personalized PageRank¶

(a)¶

In [31]:
from project1_code import question2_4a
question2_4a(alpha=0.2, num_steps=int(1e5)) # Lower alpha value

Teleport probabilities sum: 1.0000

Node with highest in-degree: 394
Node with highest visit probability: 394

Because the teleportation probability =0.2 is low, the Personalized PageRank results here (with custom teleport probabilities) are similar to our 3(a) results. Since the walker doesn't teleport often, the empirical node probabilities are similar in both problems, since the walker generally follows the directed edges in the network.

(b)¶

In [32]:
from project1_code import question2_4b
question2_4b(alpha=0.2, num_steps=int(1e5)) # Lower alpha value

Node 789 has a middle PageRank score of: 0.000272
Node 668 has a middle PageRank score of: 0.000273
The median score is: 0.000272
Median nodes: [np.int64(789), np.int64(668)]

Node with highest in-degree: 394
Node with highest visit probability: 789

Because the two median nodes are the only two teleportation hubs, everytime the walker teleports, it starts at those two nodes, and the PageRank values are highest there. The empirical probabilities elsewhere are as a result of the walker moving to connected edges from those hubs.

(c)¶

The general PageRank equation

$$ \pi(i) = (1-\alpha)\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(\frac{1}{k_{\text{out}}(j)}A_{ji}\pi(j)\right) + \frac{\alpha}{n} $$

transforms to

$$ \pi(i) = (1-\alpha)\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(\frac{1}{k_{\text{out}}(j)}A_{ji}\pi(j)\right) + \frac{\alpha}{|T|} $$

where $T$ is a set of trusted nodes. If the node $i$ is not a trusted node, the personalized PageRank equation reduces to

$$ \pi(i) = (1-\alpha)\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(\frac{1}{k_{\text{out}}(j)}A_{ji}\pi(j)\right) $$
